figures.caption: Figure 3: All figures show the exact function in black, four observations as black crosses, collocation points / the predicted f as empty circles with ±2σ confidence intervals: (a) Simple Kriging (θ̂ = 0.83, σ̂ = 0.61). (b) Co-Kriging (θ̂ = 1.46, σ̂ = 0.006) (c) Lagrangian Kriging (LOOCV optimal parameters: θ̂ = 1.12, σ̂ = 0.61.) (d) Lagrangian Kriging (minimizer of the interpolation error: θ̂ = 2.38, σ̂ = 3.91). Note that, the co-Kriging uncertainty intervals are too tiny to notice owing to the model accuracy.

Figure 12: Uncertainty in the squared magnitude of the predicted velocity vectors: (a) Simple Kriging with LOOCV optimal parameters θ̂ = 2.10 and σ̂ = 0.77, (b) Co-Kriging with LOOCV optimal parameters θ̂ = 0.93 and σ̂ = 2.48.
Figure 13: The exact flow and co-Kriging and Lagrangian Kriging predictions for the airfoils NACA 0012 (top) and NACA 2424 (bottom). Observed velocity vectors marked in red.
Figure 2: Comparing the simple Kriging (in orange) and Lagrangian Kriging prediction (in green and the blue curve) with a single point constraint.
Figure 7: Lagrangian Kriging predictions for higher-order derivatives involved in the gradient-based and Laplacian operators.
Figure 14: Comparing the uncertainty quantification (±2σ) with simple Kriging vs. Co-Kriging for the airfoils NACA 0012 (top) and NACA 2424 (bottom) respectively.
Figure 1: Demonstrating the difference between simple Kriging (in orange) and the prediction (in green and the blue curve) with a single collocation point (in blue).

Figure 8: Prediction based on random additional observations in the domain: (a) Simple Kriging without cross covariance between velocity components, (b) Simple Kriging (using ϕ), (c) Co-Kriging with continuity. Both the color map and the arrow lengths represent the magnitude of velocity.

Figure 9: Simple Kriging (using ϕ) prediction based on random additional observations in the domain for varying lengthscale parameter (flow-reversal phenomena). Once again, the color and length of the arrows are proportional to the magnitude of velocity.
Figure 6: Comparing the simple Kriging vs. derivatives based collocated co-Kriging with n = 10 observations (in black), gradient observations (in brown) and Laplacian observations (in red).

Figure 11: Comparing the predicted flows. Observed velocity vectors in red. The LOOCV optimal co-Kriging parameters, θ̂ = 0.93 and σ̂ = 2.48. The Lagrangian Kriging predictions (optimal lengthscale parameter, θ̂ = 2.01) are shown in blue, using which, the rest is a simple Kriging based interpolation.
Figure 5: The effect of increasing observations on Lagrangian Kriging prediction (in blue) without uncertainty for q = 100 prediction points. Fixed θ = 1.
Figure 4: Lagrangian Kriging with the same observations but predicting at q = 1000 locations. (a) Using the optimal LOOCV parameters. (b) Using parameters minimizing the interpolation error.
